evidence: src/lib/ has a sensible domain decomposition that improved recently: auth/, calculations/, data/, db/, server/, stores/, types/, ui/, components/. Sibling folders are coherent (e.g. lib/auth/ pairs oauthBase.ts/oauth.ts/blizzardOauth.ts and tokenCacheBase.ts/tokenCache.ts/blizzardTokenCache.ts), so a new engineer can predict where most code lives.

src/lib/db/userSchema.ts opens with a thorough doc comment explaining the intentional two-database split (DATABASE_URL vs DATABASE_USER_URL, schema.ts vs userSchema.ts, getRaidDb vs getUserDb, drizzle.config.ts vs drizzle.user.config.ts). The architectural contract for the most surprising piece of the system is written down at the source.
PRODUCT.md is concrete and trustworthy (audience, purpose, principles); it matches the routes/api/* tools shipped (calculator, raid log browser, encounter analytics).
README.md is empty (0 bytes). The top-level reference doc that should orient a new engineer is missing — the look_for explicitly calls for project contracts/reference docs matching reality.

src/lib/db/connection.ts exports createDrizzlePostgres({label, connectionString, connectionStringSource}) as a factory and its module doc says it 'owns those settings so the two getXxxDb() wrappers in index.ts and userDb.ts differ only by the env var'. grep_search('createDrizzlePostgres') returns only the definition — no caller. db/index.ts (getRaidDb) and db/userDb.ts (getUserDb) each still inline the full postgres({prepare:false, max:1, idle_timeout:5, ...}) config. The factory is dead code; ownership of 'how we connect to Postgres' is split across three files.

src/lib/db/index.ts is conceptually 'the raid db entry point' (defines and exports getRaidDb) but also re-exports user-domain modules: `export * from './userSchema'; export * from './users'; export * from './userRecents'; export { getUserDb } from './userDb';`. The dual-DB story documented in userSchema.ts is then conflated by a single index that hides the boundary.

src/routes/api/ uses three coexisting naming patterns: resource-events (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, death-events, healing-events), verb-resource / mixed-noun (browse-logs, calculate-runs, current-state, character-reports, character-score, recent-reports, recent-characters, my-wow-characters, player-details, fights, damage-average, death-hotspots, top-players, spec-top-players, spec-statistics), and provider passthroughs (blizzard, raiderio). A new engineer cannot predict the URL for a new endpoint.

Two component roots: src/components/ (feature components: charts/, calculator/, combobox/, layout/, raid/, skeletons/, seo.svelte, authButton.svelte) and src/lib/components/ui/ (shadcn primitives only). The split is defensible (feature vs primitive) but is not documented anywhere; SvelteKit convention is to keep components under $lib, and src/components/ violates that without explanation.

Two utility homes: src/lib/utils.ts (top-level file, exports `cn`, `WithoutChild`, etc. — shadcn boilerplate) coexists with src/lib/utils/ (directory containing only clientLog.ts). The historical 'utils mixes 17 files across 5+ unrelated domains' was reorganized (wontfix), but the residue is two same-named utility surfaces that newcomers will conflate.

Top-level src/ contains auth.ts, stores.ts, hooks.server.ts, test-setup.ts, test-utils.ts. auth.ts and hooks.server.ts are SvelteKit conventions (fine); stores.ts at src/ root coexists with src/lib/stores/ (recentCharacters.ts, recentReports.ts, ...) — two store homes with no obvious dividing line.

Near-empty subsystem folders: src/lib/hooks/ is empty, src/lib/actions/ has only reveal.ts. These directories suggest incomplete reorganization or speculative scaffolding; their existence implies categories that aren't really populated.

scan_evidence.dead_code lists ~30 orphaned files. Most are src/routes/api/*/+server.ts which are SvelteKit file-system entry points (not real orphans). But src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts (151 LOC) and src/lib/db/userCharacters.ts (99 LOC) are non-route lib modules with no importers — domain code whose ownership and purpose are now undefined.
src/lib/types/realms.ts is 572 LOC (top of large_file_distribution). It is a flat data table, not a complexity hotspot, so it doesn't materially harm decomposition; noted as background.

docs/MIGRATION_PLAN.md describes a baseline of 'Svelte 4.2.20, Tailwind 3, Zod 3, Vite 5' as 'state of main after Safe-A audit'. The repo's package.json/code may have moved past that baseline; if so, the plan document is drifting from runtime reality (project-contracts trustworthiness signal).

src/lib/server/logger.ts and src/lib/server/apiResponses.ts define a deliberate trio (logServerError / handleApiError / apiError) with documented contract and stable [scope] prefixes; most route handlers under src/routes/api/* use one of these so the wire shape ({error: string}) and server log shape are consistent.

src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts introduces a typed WclQueryError with kind: 'http'|'graphql' and a centralized handleWclFightRequest() that maps WCL transport vs GraphQL vs unknown failures to 502/500 with one log call — a designed boundary error model.
src/lib/db/_helpers.ts dbOperation wraps every DB call: logs with [db] label and rethrows; the doc explicitly forbids translating errors to empty values. db/ functions consistently use this wrapper.

INCONSISTENT: API handlers split between two equivalent-but-different idioms: handleApiError(scope, err, msg) (browse-logs, damage-average, death-hotspots, top-players, spec-statistics, spec-top-players) vs manually paired logServerError(...) + apiError(...) (current-state, recent-reports, recent-characters, player-details, fights, death-events, blizzard, character-reports). Identical net behavior, two parallel code paths.

DEGRADATION HIDING: src/routes/api/character-reports/+server.ts top-level catch logs and then returns apiOk({reports: [], guilds: []}) — a thrown error becomes a success response with empty data, exactly the anti-pattern dbOperation's contract docs warn against. Inner catches (fetchReportsForCharacter / fetchReportsForGuild / bnet roster) similarly degrade to [] on failure with no client-visible signal.

EMPTY CATCH + WEAK LOGGING: src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts refreshBattleNetToken bypasses dbOperation, has a fetch try/catch that logs only a literal string with no error context (`console.error('battlenet: refresh fetch failed')`), and an empty `catch {}` that silently drops DB persistence failures of a freshly refreshed token (comment 'Ignore persistence failures here').
src/lib/data/warcraftlogs.ts catches errors as `_err` / bare `catch {}` and returns null with NO logging — failures are completely invisible.
src/lib/data/characterData.ts has two `catch {}` blocks (lines 57, 104) that swallow without logging.

Logging-channel drift: src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts and src/lib/stores/recentCharactersBackends.ts / recentReportsBackends.ts use bare `console.error('module: ...', error)` strings instead of logServerError, even when running server-side. Different log shape ('myWowRoster: region X failed' vs '[scope] message').

RETURN-TYPE STRATEGY MIX for similar reads: stores/* `loadFromAPI` returns `null` on any failure; api/character-reports returns empty arrays; db/* throws via dbOperation; data/warcraftlogs returns null. No documented per-layer convention beyond _helpers.ts comment.

CONTEXT LOSS: src/routes/api/blizzard/+server.ts catch returns `error.message` to the client (apiError(error.message)) — leaks internal error text, while sibling handlers return generic 'Internal Server Error.' Inconsistent client-facing message policy.

STATUS CODE INCONSISTENCY: handleWclFightRequest returns 502 for WCL failures; equivalent WCL failure paths in death-events/fights/character-reports return 500 (default apiError) instead of 502. Same upstream-failure semantics, different HTTP signal.

POSITIVE: src/auth.ts wraps all best-effort callbacks (signIn token update, background roster sync, session profile upsert, last_seen) in try/catch that calls logServerError with descriptive scope+message — the previously-flagged 'empty catch' pattern is no longer present.

POSITIVE: WclQueryError is the only custom error class but it's the one place where call sites genuinely branch on error kind (character-reports/logWclFailure splits graphql vs other into warn vs error).
Module-level I/O at hooks.server.ts line 43 (cookie read in handle) is inside the framework handler, not import-time — not an error-handling defect.

Strong, consistent conventions: camelCase functions, descriptive domain nouns (getRaidStats, extractWarcraftLogsReportCode, raiderIoRealmCandidates, keystoneCalculations, hideWowheadTooltips), and clear get/is/extract/format/configure prefixes throughout src/lib and src/components.

Svelte event handlers consistently use the handle* prefix (handleSignIn, handleSelect, handlePageChange, handleBossChange, handleReportSelection, handleFightSelection, handleLogSearch). This is a well-known framework convention used 10+ times — treated as established convention, not a generic-verb smell.

Server error helper handleApiError (src/lib/server/logger.ts:42) and handleWclFightRequest (src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts:90) are domain-specific despite the handle* prefix; the suffix carries the intent (ApiError, WclFightRequest), so naming is honest.

Most prior process*/temp* offenders are gone: processSeriesData, processData (formerly damageChart.svelte), and tempScore (formerly keystoneCalculations.ts) no longer appear in src/. Only processLargeDataset remains in src/lib/__tests__/performance.test.ts, which is a non-production test fixture (out of scoring zone).

doSave callback still present at src/lib/data/currentState.ts:42 — wraps the actual fetch+error logging. The 'do' prefix adds no information; the function literally is the save (a name like persistState or sendSaveRequest would be clearer).

Local `hide` arrow at src/lib/ui/wowheadTooltips.ts:48 inside hideWowheadTooltips is a one-line shortcut for 'hideTooltipApis + hideTooltipElements'. As a tiny private closure used 3 times in the same scope it is acceptable, but a name like flushHide or hideAll would communicate the multi-step nature.

Abbreviation drift: codebase strongly prefers `response` for fetch results (hooks.server.ts, oauthBase.ts, bossPreviewChart.svelte, averageChart.svelte, yourReports.svelte, raiderio/+server.ts — 7+ occurrences). One outlier uses `res`: src/components/charts/deathHotspots.svelte:36 (`const res = await fetch(...)` then `res.json()`). The previously flagged res in routes/api/character-score/+server.ts is gone (file no longer present).

Generic short locals (`val`, `data`, `result`) appear but are scoped tightly (single-statement transforms inside chart tooltip callbacks, DB query mappers) where the surrounding context fully disambiguates — consistent with codebase norm rather than a defect pattern.

Storage/utility module names are intent-revealing: myWowCharacters, recentReports, raiderIoRealmCandidates, extractWarcraftLogsReportCode, titleCaseWords. No name/behavior mismatch observed (no isX returning non-boolean, no getX with mutation in inspected files).
File names track responsibility: currentState.ts, wowheadTooltips.ts, keystoneCalculations.ts, logger.ts, wclGraphQL.ts — concrete and discoverable.

src/lib/auth/oauthBase.ts and src/lib/auth/tokenCacheBase.ts are earned shared abstractions: requestClientCredentialsToken() + createTokenCache() factory each have two real callers (WCL and Battle.net) at src/lib/auth/oauth.ts, blizzardOauth.ts, tokenCache.ts, blizzardTokenCache.ts. The previous duplication finding is genuinely resolved.

src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts exposes executeWclQuery + parseFightRequestBody + handleWclFightRequest. The factory is consumed by cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, and boss-events route handlers (4 callers, real polymorphic substitution via the options object), so the abstraction pays for itself.

Holistic abstractions evidence shows wrapper_rate 0.026, total_wrappers 1, one_impl_interface_count 0, facade_module_count 0, typed_dict_violation_count 0, enum_bypass_count 0 — almost no ceremonial indirection across the codebase.
The single detected pass-through wrapper, cn() in src/lib/utils.ts, intentionally composes clsx + twMerge — this is the canonical shadcn-svelte utility, not gratuitous indirection.

src/lib/components/ui/* button/index.ts etc. are barrel re-export facades, but they are intentional package entry points for a third-party-style UI kit (shadcn-svelte), which the rubric explicitly excludes.

Outlier: src/routes/api/death-events/+server.ts uses parseFightRequestBody + executeWclQuery + bespoke try/catch instead of handleWclFightRequest, despite having the exact body shape and error mapping that the factory was designed for — a genuine consistency gap rather than added behavior.

wide_param_bags signals are false positives in this codebase: chartCanvasPlugin.ts dereferences a Chart.js plugin context object (framework contract), and browse-logs/+server.ts dereferences a typed BrowseLogsParams request body inside a single handler — neither passes a bag through layers.

indirection_hotspots are mostly chain depth 3 inside individual route handlers (parse → execute → transform), not cross-feature wrapper chains; max chain depth 6 is concentrated in one file and reflects sequential local steps, not stacked indirection.

Generic options on handleWclFightRequest<TData, TBody> are exercised with multiple distinct concrete pairs (CastEventsData, DamageGraphData, HealingGraphData, BossEventsData), so the generic surface is justified.
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: death-events/+server.ts bypasses handleWclFightRequest, leaving one feature inconsistent with the established abstraction; otherwise the abstraction layer is unusually clean for a project this size.

evidence: requireSession (src/lib/server/requireSession.ts) + apiError/apiOk (src/lib/server/apiResponses.ts) define a single, narrow auth seam: every protected /api/* handler I sampled (recent-characters, recent-reports, current-state, my-wow-characters) follows the exact same `const auth = await requireSession(locals); if ('response' in auth) return auth.response; try { ... } catch (e) { logServerError(...); return apiError(...) }` shape

handleWclFightRequest (src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts) collapses 5 per-fight endpoints (damage-events, healing-events, death-events, cast-events, boss-events) into ~30-line files by composing query + transform around a shared body parser, query runner, and error→Response translator (e.g. src/routes/api/damage-events/+server.ts is just a query string + transform)

WclQueryError carries a typed `kind: 'http' | 'graphql'` discriminant and is caught once at the seam (src/routes/api/fights/+server.ts maps to 502 vs 500); error propagation across the WCL boundary is predictable

createTokenCache (src/lib/auth/tokenCacheBase.ts) factors the WCL and Battle.net token caches into one closure with documented concurrency contract; tokenCache.ts and blizzardTokenCache.ts each just supply provider config

Recent-reports and recent-characters store backends (src/lib/stores/recentReportsBackends.ts, recentCharactersBackends.ts) expose a discriminated AddOutcome union ('ok'|'unauthorized'|'unavailable') so the composition layer (recentReports.ts) reacts to backend-specific failure modes via a small switch — clean, explicit boundary contract

characterData.ts uses discriminated FetchRunsResult / FetchWowSummaryResult unions ({kind:'ok'|'empty'|'error',status}) and is documented as framework-agnostic so callers wire stores at the call site rather than threading them across the seam

Defect: src/lib/db/connection.ts exports `createDrizzlePostgres({label, connectionString, connectionStringSource})` and its module doc states the two getXxxDb wrappers should differ only by env var + label — but neither caller uses it. getRaidDb (src/lib/db/index.ts) and getUserDb (src/lib/db/userDb.ts) each inline a verbatim copy of the full postgres client config (prepare:false, max:1, idle_timeout:5, max_lifetime:30, types:{}, ...). The seam exists but is bypassed

Defect: src/hooks.server.ts handleSession resolves event.locals.getSession by issuing `fetch(event.url.origin + '/auth/session', { headers: { cookie: ... }})` — a self-HTTP round trip back into the same SvelteKit app to read the session, instead of calling Auth.js directly. This crosses an unnecessary network seam on every request and forces re-serialising the cookie across it

Defect: src/routes/api/browse-logs/+server.ts casts `await request.json()` directly to `BrowseLogsParams` with no validation, while sibling per-fight handlers go through `parseFightRequestBody` and `fights/+server.ts` does explicit `typeof code !== 'string'` checks before trusting the body. Boundary-validation contract is inconsistent across siblings

Minor seam-overload: src/routes/api/current-state/+server.ts POST embeds title/subtitle synthesis from URL params alongside auth + persistence at the API seam — translation logic that could live with the consumer; not pulled into a finding because it is localised to a single handler

Only 11 test files exist for ~104 production source files; the entire src/routes/api/* tree (22 +server.ts handlers) has zero tests despite handling auth, payments-adjacent score lookups, and external API integration (Warcraft Logs, Blizzard, Raider.io).

src/lib/calculations/keystoneCalculations.ts contains the core rating/score formula that the rating-calculator route depends on — there is no corresponding keystoneCalculations.test.ts, so the central business calculation has no regression coverage.

src/lib/auth/* (blizzardOauth.ts, oauth.ts, oauthBase.ts, tokenCache.ts, blizzardTokenCache.ts) — token caching, refresh, and OAuth bootstrap — has no tests; src/hooks.server.ts (CSP + session forwarding) is also untested.

src/lib/db/userDb.ts, src/lib/db/schema.ts, src/lib/db/userSchema.ts, src/lib/db/_helpers.ts — the data access layer (5+ importers each) has only a one-line existence test in src/lib/__tests__/dbIndex.test.ts: `expect(typeof getRaidDb).toBe('function')`.

src/lib/__tests__/performance.test.ts is structurally junk: it defines its own `heavyComputation`, `processLargeDataset`, `simulateApiCall` fixtures inside the test file and asserts arbitrary wall-clock thresholds (<1000ms, <500ms, <200ms) — it validates nothing in the production code and is environment/timing fragile.

src/lib/__tests__/drizzleConfigs.test.ts asserts the literal `schema`, `out`, `dialect` strings of drizzle.config.ts and drizzle.user.config.ts — pure shape tautology that breaks on any directory rename without catching a real defect.
src/lib/__tests__/formIndex.test.ts only verifies that the barrel module `$lib/components/ui/form` re-exports specific names and aliases — it tests the index file's mirror of itself, not behavior.

src/components/__tests__/contentShowcase.test.ts uses `vi.useFakeTimers()` in beforeEach but the rendered component is static (no timers in the asserted paths) — coupling to internal styling: asserts class strings like `hover:scale-105`, `transition-transform`, `block`, `h-auto` that change with any Tailwind tweak without indicating a regression.

src/components/__tests__/page.test.ts asserts marketing copy verbatim ('Master World of Warcraft with Mr. Mythical', 'Premium WoW Tools for Competitive Players') — these tests will break on every copy edit and provide no behavioral guarantee.

Mechanical scan reports 70 test_coverage findings; combined with the orphaned-file overlap on every API route (`detectors: [boilerplate_duplication, orphaned, smells, test_coverage]`) the missing coverage is concentrated on the highest-fan-in production files.

There are no integration tests: no tests exercise a SvelteKit endpoint via `+server.ts` handlers, no tests stub fetch to verify upstream Warcraft Logs / Blizzard error handling, and no tests cover the load functions in src/routes/rating-calculator/+page.server.ts.

The remaining genuinely useful tests are narrow: warcraftlogs.test.ts (one pure parser, ~5 cases), abilityMetadata.test.ts (pure helpers), interactionUtils.test.ts (reveal action), utils.test.ts (cn wrapper), dungeonCombobox.test.ts (one component) — covering only a small fraction of the surface.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has a recognisable domain decomposition (auth, data, db, server, stores, types, ui, calculations) and one well-documented architectural contract (the two-database split in userSchema.ts), so most of a new engineer's questions about 'where does X live?' have a believable answer. However, several ownership boundaries remain blurred: db/index.ts conflates the raid- and user-DB roles via barrel re-exports, db/connection.ts is a factory module nobody calls, src/components/ and src/lib/components/ui/ are two unannotated component homes, src/lib/utils.ts and src/lib/utils/ are two utility homes with the same name, and routes/api/* uses three different naming schemes. The README is empty, which is the single biggest signal that the project's stated contracts are not yet trustworthy.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Score sits between 'clear with minor blur' (90) and 'struggle to explain' (80), pulled toward the latter by concrete, falsifiable confusions a newcomer would hit on day one — empty README, dead factory module duplicated inline twice, mixed db/index.ts role, three API naming patterns, twin component/util homes. The decomposition itself is sound and improving (recent wontfix entries mark prior reorganization as done), so 73 reflects 'mostly defensible structure with several real ownership-blur defects' rather than the muddled 60 anchor.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The server core has a thoughtfully designed error spine — logServerError/handleApiError/apiError, dbOperation, and WclQueryError form a coherent layered contract with documented intent — and most API routes honor it with matching shapes. However, the contract is undermined at the edges: two equivalent idioms compete for the same handler shape, several modules (data/warcraftlogs.ts, data/characterData.ts, parts of bnetAccount.ts) silently swallow errors with empty catches and no logging, and character-reports actively converts thrown errors into empty-success responses, defeating the very 'errors stay observable' rule the dbOperation docs encode.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase shows real architectural intent around errors (typed WCL boundary, centralized helpers, documented DB contract, recovered auth.ts), which lifts it well above ad-hoc handling, but consistent application is missing: silent swallowing in client/data layers, two parallel handler idioms, mixed return-on-failure strategies (null/[]/throw) for similar reads, an explicit success-on-error fallback in character-reports, and at least one client-leaking error message hold the score in the upper-mid range rather than the high-quality band.

naming_quality.dimension_character: Naming is a clear strength: conventions are uniform across modules, domain vocabulary is rich and specific, and the dominant prefixes (get/is/extract/format/handle/configure) are applied consistently enough that they read as a project dialect rather than generic verbs. A few isolated low-information names persist (one doSave callback, one stray `res` abbreviation, a tiny local `hide`), but nothing systemic and most previously flagged offenders have already been cleaned up.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Above-average code with idiomatic, intent-revealing names earns the 75–90 band, with anchor weight pulled toward the upper end because remaining defects are isolated single-symbol issues with trivial fixes. The score is held below 85 by the lingering doSave/res inconsistencies and the absence of explicit naming guidelines that would prevent future drift.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The codebase keeps abstractions earned and minimal: shared OAuth/token-cache factories each serve two providers, and a single WCL GraphQL handler factory cleanly absorbs validation/error mapping for the per-fight event endpoints. Indirection hotspots and 'wide param bag' signals reduce, on inspection, to local request-body parsing and Chart.js framework contracts rather than ceremonial wrappers, and there are no one-implementation interfaces or pass-through type shells of note.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Anchors at the 90-tier (mostly strong with a few layers that feel slightly overbuilt but don't slow the developer) — every shared abstraction has multiple real callers and the only consistency miss is death-events not adopting the WCL fight-request factory. The score sits a hair under 90 because that miss is visible to anyone reading the per-fight endpoints side by side.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Boundary design is the strongest sustained quality dimension in this codebase: the API surface, auth, WCL GraphQL, OAuth token caches, and store/backend split each expose narrow, discriminated, well-documented seams that compose cleanly (`requireSession`, `apiError/apiOk`, `handleWclFightRequest`, `createTokenCache`, `AddOutcome`/`FetchRunsResult` unions). Friction shows up in three localised places — an extracted DB-connection factory that callers ignore, a self-HTTP hop for session retrieval inside hooks.server.ts, and one route-handler that skips the validation contract its siblings honour — so a developer mostly trusts the seams but occasionally hits a boundary that betrays the prevailing pattern.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The dominant experience is 'good seams with minor friction at a few boundaries' (anchor ~90) rather than the 'mostly well-served but recurring friction' of 80, but the bypassed `createDrizzlePostgres` factory and the cross-process self-fetch in hooks.server.ts are visible enough — and the validation inconsistency at browse-logs concrete enough — to pull this below the 90 anchor. I land at 81.5 because the issues are real and recurring (one per major seam family) yet none are structural; they are isolated against a backdrop of unusually deliberate seam composition.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test portfolio is sparse and skewed: a handful of focused unit tests cover pure helpers (warcraftlogs report code, ability metadata, the cn class utility, the reveal action) while the entire API surface, OAuth/token layer, server hooks, database access, and the keystone scoring engine — i.e. the actual product — are completely uncovered. Several existing tests are anti-tests: a performance suite asserts wall-clock thresholds against in-file fabricated workloads, a drizzle-config test re-states config string literals, a form-index test re-states barrel exports, and component tests assert marketing copy and Tailwind class names that fragment on any edit. A developer making changes to scoring math, an API handler, or auth would have no automated safety net and could not trust the green CI signal as evidence of correctness.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Per the global anchors this falls in the 20-40 band: meaningful risk routinely goes unvalidated and several tests are actively misleading (timing-dependent perf checks, copy/class string assertions). It is not a 20 because there are a few small, well-written unit tests that do validate real behavior; it is well below 60 because the developer cannot rely on the suite to catch regressions in business-critical paths. Score ~32 reflects high confidence that the strategy has systemic blind spots rather than isolated gaps.

issues.summary: README.md is empty — top-level project contract is missing
issues.suggestion: Write a short README.md (one screen) that links to PRODUCT.md, names the two databases (DATABASE_URL raid analytics, DATABASE_USER_URL user/auth) and points at userSchema.ts for the dual-DB rationale, and lists the top-level src/ areas (lib/auth, lib/data, lib/db, lib/server, lib/stores, lib/ui, components, routes/api). The goal is a 'where does X live?' map, not prose.
evidence: README.md is 0 bytes (read returned 'file exists, but is empty').
PRODUCT.md and DESIGN.md exist but neither is a README; PRODUCT.md is brand/audience, DESIGN.md is a design-tokens YAML.
There is no top-level orientation explaining the two-database split, the two component roots, or the api route taxonomy.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/connection.ts factory is dead code; getRaidDb and getUserDb still duplicate it inline
issues.suggestion: Either adopt the factory (rewrite getRaidDb / getUserDb to two ~5-line wrappers calling createDrizzlePostgres with their respective label and env var) or delete connection.ts. Today's state — three modules claiming ownership of the same connection contract — is the worst of both options.
evidence: connection.ts exports createDrizzlePostgres({label, connectionString, connectionStringSource}) and its doc comment claims it 'owns those settings so the two getXxxDb() wrappers in index.ts and userDb.ts differ only by the env var'.
Workspace search for 'createDrizzlePostgres' returns only the definition — no importer.

index.ts:7-50 inlines the full postgres({prepare:false, max:1, idle_timeout:5, connect_timeout:10, transform:undefined, types:{}, onnotice:()=>{}, debug:false, fetch_types:false, max_lifetime:30}) configuration; userDb.ts:6-50 repeats the identical block. The 'shared owner' module exists but nobody owns it.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/index.ts barrel-exports user-DB modules, conflating the documented two-database boundary
issues.suggestion: Make db/index.ts the raid-DB entry only (define/export getRaidDb and re-export schema.ts), and make consumers import user-DB things from a sibling barrel like '$lib/db/user' (which would re-export userSchema, users, userRecents, getUserDb). Mirrors the dual-config split (drizzle.config.ts vs drizzle.user.config.ts) at the import level.
evidence: userSchema.ts opens with a doc comment carefully separating raidSchema (DATABASE_URL, getRaidDb) from userSchema (DATABASE_USER_URL, getUserDb) — 'one of two intentionally separate databases'.

index.ts then collapses both into one entry point: `export * from './userSchema'; export * from './users'; export * from './userRecents'; export { getUserDb } from './userDb';` while also defining and exporting getRaidDb itself.

The result: a single $lib/db import surface for two databases the rest of the architecture treats as deliberately separate. Any consumer doing `import { getRaidDb, getUserDb, users } from '$lib/db'` undermines the boundary the schema files describe.

issues.summary: routes/api uses three naming patterns (resource-events, verb-resource, provider-passthrough) with no documented rule
issues.suggestion: Pick one rule and document it in the (currently empty) README. A reasonable split: external-provider proxies under api/proxy/{blizzard,raiderio,wcl}/, all other endpoints as resource-noun (api/<resource>/<aggregation-or-events>). Don't create barrels or wrappers; just rename folders and update imports. Even keeping today's three patterns is acceptable IF the README explains when to use each.
evidence: Resource-noun group: boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, death-events, healing-events.

Verb-resource / mixed-noun group: browse-logs, calculate-runs, current-state, character-reports, character-score, recent-reports, recent-characters, my-wow-characters, player-details, fights, damage-average, death-hotspots, top-players, spec-top-players, spec-statistics.
Provider-passthrough group: blizzard/, raiderio/ (named after the upstream API rather than the resource served).
No README or DESIGN file documents which pattern applies to which kind of endpoint.

issues.summary: src/components/ and src/lib/components/ui/ are two component roots with no documented dividing line
issues.suggestion: Either document the rule in README ('feature components live in src/components/<feature>/, low-level UI primitives live in $lib/components/ui/ — shadcn import path is preserved') or move src/components/ under $lib/components/feature/ to match the convention. Do not introduce a barrel.
evidence: src/components/ holds feature components (charts/, calculator/, combobox/, layout/, raid/, skeletons/, seo.svelte, authButton.svelte).
src/lib/components/ui/ holds shadcn primitives (accordion/, badge/, button/, ..., textarea/) and nothing else.
SvelteKit convention is to keep components under $lib; src/components/ deviates without explanation.

Concrete consequence: feature components routinely cross the boundary via `import Button from '$lib/components/ui/button/button.svelte'` (e.g. components/authButton.svelte, components/layout/header.svelte, components/charts/damageChart.svelte). The split works in practice but a new engineer cannot derive the rule.

issues.summary: src/lib/utils.ts and src/lib/utils/ coexist as same-named utility surfaces
issues.suggestion: Move src/lib/utils.ts contents into src/lib/utils/cn.ts (or src/lib/utils/index.ts) and delete the top-level utils.ts, leaving exactly one $lib/utils home. Or, conversely, move clientLog.ts up and remove the directory. Pick one.
evidence: src/lib/utils.ts (14 LOC) holds shadcn helpers (cn, WithoutChild, WithoutChildren, WithElementRef).
src/lib/utils/ (directory) holds only clientLog.ts.

TypeScript will treat `import { foo } from '$lib/utils'` and `import { foo } from '$lib/utils/clientLog'` as essentially the same namespace; the file-vs-directory split with the same name creates an obvious 'where do I add a new utility?' ambiguity.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts and src/lib/db/userCharacters.ts have zero importers — domain code with undefined ownership
issues.suggestion: For each: either delete it (if the feature was abandoned), wire it up to the caller it was written for (if mid-flight), or move it into a __scratch__/ folder with a README explaining the deferred work. Today they are silent ownership debt that makes db/ look bigger than it really is.
evidence: scan_evidence.dead_code flags both as orphaned (151 LOC and 99 LOC respectively).
Both sit inside src/lib/db/ next to actively-used modules (users.ts, userRecents.ts), so visually they read as part of the user-DB story, but no caller exists.
Routes/+server.ts orphans on the same list are SvelteKit file-system entrypoints (legitimately importer-less); these two are not.

issues.summary: src/lib/hooks/ and src/lib/actions/ are empty/near-empty, implying scaffolding that never landed
issues.suggestion: If the categories are intentional, add a one-line README in each explaining 'this folder holds Svelte actions / runes-style hooks and is intentionally small'. If not, delete the empty hooks/ folder and inline reveal.ts where it's actually used.
evidence: list_dir(src/lib/hooks) returns 'Folder is empty'.
src/lib/actions/ contains only reveal.ts.
Their presence implies the categories 'hooks' and 'actions' are owned subsystems, but they are not yet populated; a new engineer cannot tell whether to add new code there or somewhere else.

issues.summary: character-reports top-level catch converts thrown errors into apiOk({reports:[], guilds:[]}), hiding failures from clients and contradicting the dbOperation contract
issues.suggestion: Return apiError(...) with an appropriate status (or include a `degraded`/`partialFailure` flag in the success payload) when the top-level catch fires; for inner catches, surface a per-source warning array so the client can render a banner instead of an apparently-empty result.
evidence: Lines ~300-303: `} catch (error) { logServerError(...); return apiOk({ reports: [], guilds: [] } satisfies CharacterReportsResponse); }`
Inner catches (fetchReportsForCharacter line ~130, fetchReportsForGuild line ~150, bnet roster fetch line ~190) also degrade silently to [] without exposing any 'partial' flag.

src/lib/db/_helpers.ts header explicitly states: 'Errors are never silently translated to those empty values — callers are responsible for catching and degrading UX ... so partial failures stay observable.'

issues.summary: API handlers split between handleApiError() and a manually paired logServerError + apiError; both produce the same shape but exist as parallel patterns
issues.suggestion: Pick one idiom. handleApiError already exists and is the documented helper — migrate the manual pairs to it, or add a lint rule that disallows logServerError directly inside a route handler's outermost catch.
evidence: handleApiError used in: browse-logs, damage-average, death-hotspots, top-players, spec-statistics, spec-top-players.
Manual logServerError(...) + apiError('...') used in: current-state, recent-reports, recent-characters, player-details, fights, death-events, blizzard, character-reports.
Both produce identical wire output ({error: string}, 500), but reviewers can't grep for one canonical 'API caught error' pattern.

issues.summary: Several non-DB modules swallow errors with empty/anonymous catches and no logging, making failures invisible
issues.suggestion: Route every catch through logServerError (or a client-equivalent if running in the browser) and always pass the error object, not just a string. For the bnetAccount empty catch, at least log the persistence failure; for the data-layer null returns, log once and consider returning a tagged Result so callers can distinguish 'not found' from 'lookup failed'.
evidence: src/lib/data/warcraftlogs.ts lines 6-15: `catch (_err)` and inner `catch {}` both `return null` with zero logging.
src/lib/data/characterData.ts lines 57, 104: bare `} catch {}` blocks with no logging or signal.

src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts line ~98: empty `catch {}` for DB persistence of a freshly refreshed Battle.net token, with comment 'Ignore persistence failures here' — token refresh succeeds but next request will refetch unnecessarily and the symptom is invisible.
src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts line ~67: `console.error('battlenet: refresh fetch failed')` logs a literal string with NO error object — stack trace is dropped.

issues.summary: Server-side modules outside src/lib/server/ and src/lib/db/ bypass logServerError and emit free-form console.error strings, breaking the documented log shape
issues.suggestion: Adopt logServerError for any code path that runs on the server (myWowRoster, bnetAccount). For client-only stores, define a parallel clientLog helper (one already exists at src/lib/utils/clientLog.ts but is unused) and route all browser logs through it with the same [scope] convention.
evidence: logger.ts contract: '[scope] message' with a stable scope tag — designed for grepping all errors from a route.
myWowRoster.ts uses `console.error('myWowRoster: region ${region} failed', error)` — different prefix style, not greppable as a [scope].
stores/recentCharactersBackends.ts and recentReportsBackends.ts use `console.error('Error loading recent characters from API:', error)` — free-form English strings.
bnetAccount.ts uses `console.error('battlenet: ...')` strings inline.

issues.summary: WCL upstream failures return 502 from handleWclFightRequest but 500 from sibling handlers that inline their own try/catch
issues.suggestion: Migrate all WCL-using handlers to handleWclFightRequest (or a sibling helper for non-fight queries) so 'upstream WCL failed' consistently maps to 502.
evidence: wclGraphQL.ts handleWclFightRequest: `if (error instanceof WclQueryError) return apiError(options.fetchErrorMessage, 502);`
death-events/+server.ts and fights/+server.ts catch WclQueryError-shaped errors but call `apiError('Failed to fetch ... from API.')` without status, defaulting to 500.
character-reports never branches on WclQueryError at the top level.

issues.summary: api/blizzard returns the raw error.message to the client, while sibling handlers return generic strings — inconsistent and an info-leak risk
issues.suggestion: Replace with `handleApiError('api/blizzard', error)` to log the detail server-side and return the standard generic message to the client.
evidence: Line 47: `return apiError(error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal Server Error.');`
Sibling handlers (death-events, fights, player-details, current-state) all return a fixed sanitized string.

issues.reasoning: Code now uses logServerError for both the token update and the background sync IIFE; the empty-catch pattern referenced by the historical issue no longer exists.

issues.summary: doSave callback uses generic 'do' prefix that adds no semantic information
issues.suggestion: Rename to `persistState` or `sendSaveRequest`, or inline the IIFE as `void (async () => { ... })()` since the closure is invoked exactly once at the end of saveToAPI.
evidence: src/lib/data/currentState.ts:42 declares `const doSave = async () => { ... }` whose body is the actual POST to /api/current-state with error logging.
The wrapper exists only because saveToAPI is sync but needs to fire-and-forget an async call; the 'do' adds nothing the surrounding saveToAPI name does not already imply.

issues.summary: `res` abbreviation diverges from project convention `response` in deathHotspots fetch
issues.suggestion: Rename `res` → `response` in deathHotspots.svelte to match the established convention; this is a 2-occurrence rename with no callers outside the local scope.
evidence: src/components/charts/deathHotspots.svelte:36 uses `const res = await fetch(...)` followed by `await res.json()` at line 41.

Sibling files consistently use `response`: bossPreviewChart.svelte:105, averageChart.svelte:244, yourReports.svelte:68, hooks.server.ts:8/41, oauthBase.ts:18, routes/api/raiderio/+server.ts:41 — making `res` the lone outlier in the chart components.

issues.summary: Local `hide` arrow inside hideWowheadTooltips obscures that it runs both API and DOM clears
issues.suggestion: Rename the local closure to something like `purge` or `runHidePass` so the three repeated invocations communicate the multi-step intent at the call sites.
evidence: src/lib/ui/wowheadTooltips.ts:48 defines `const hide = () => { hideTooltipApis(...); hideTooltipElements(...); }` and invokes it three times (immediate, on rAF, on setTimeout).
The name `hide` collides with the wrapping function name `hideWowheadTooltips` and obscures that it is a composed two-step purge, not a single hide operation.

issues.summary: death-events/+server.ts duplicates handleWclFightRequest plumbing instead of calling the shared factory.
issues.suggestion: Refactor death-events/+server.ts to call handleWclFightRequest<DeathsData, { deathEvents: DeathEvent[] }>(request, { query: QUERY, operation: 'api/death-events', fetchErrorMessage: 'Failed to fetch death events from API.', transform: (data) => ({ deathEvents: ... }) }), removing the local try/catch and parseFightRequestBody usage.
evidence: death-events imports executeWclQuery + parseFightRequestBody + WclQueryError and re-implements: parseFightRequestBody check → 400, executeWclQuery call with the standard {code, fightID, start, end} variables, try/catch mapping WclQueryError→502 and other errors→500.

Sibling endpoints cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, boss-events all delegate the same shape to handleWclFightRequest<TData, TBody>(request, { query, operation, fetchErrorMessage, transform }).

death-events' transform step (filter + actor lookup) fits the existing transform: (data, body) => result signature with no additional behavior, auth, or telemetry that would justify the bespoke handler.

issues.summary: createDrizzlePostgres factory in src/lib/db/connection.ts is unused; both DB wrappers inline the full client config
issues.suggestion: Replace the bodies of getRaidDb (src/lib/db/index.ts) and getUserDb (src/lib/db/userDb.ts) with a call to createDrizzlePostgres: `return createDrizzlePostgres({ label: 'raid', connectionString: env.DATABASE_URL, connectionStringSource: 'DATABASE_URL' })` and `{ label: 'user', connectionString: env.DATABASE_USER_URL || env.DATABASE_URL, connectionStringSource: 'DATABASE_USER_URL or DATABASE_URL' }` respectively. Drop the duplicated postgres({...}) literal and try/catch from both files
evidence: src/lib/db/connection.ts exports `createDrizzlePostgres({label, connectionString, connectionStringSource})` and its module doc explicitly says the raid/user wrappers should differ only by env var + log label

src/lib/db/index.ts getRaidDb() inlines the full postgres client config (prepare:false, max:1, idle_timeout:5, connect_timeout:10, transform:undefined, types:{}, onnotice:()=>{}, debug:false, fetch_types:false, max_lifetime:30) and a try/catch with its own console.error message
src/lib/db/userDb.ts getUserDb() inlines the same block verbatim with a different env-var lookup and a different error string
Net effect: the seam is duplicated three times (factory + two callers) and any future tweak to the client config has to be made in three places, even though the codebase already paid for the factory

issues.summary: hooks.server.ts reads the session by self-fetching /auth/session over HTTP instead of calling Auth.js directly
issues.suggestion: Replace the self-fetch in handleSession with a direct call to Auth.js's session helper. With @auth/sveltekit, expose the auth() helper from src/auth.ts and call it from inside the hook (e.g. `event.locals.getSession = () => auth(event)`), or fold the session resolution into the existing handleAuth so locals.session is populated in one pass. Eliminates the HTTP boundary and the cookie hand-off
evidence: src/hooks.server.ts handleSession sets event.locals.getSession to a closure that issues `fetch(`${event.url.origin}/auth/session`, { headers: { cookie: event.request.headers.get('cookie') || '' } })` and parses the JSON response
Auth.js (./auth) is already imported at the top of hooks.server.ts as the auth handler, so the session is reachable in-process without crossing the network seam

Every protected handler that calls `await locals.getSession()` (via requireSession) therefore pays a re-entrant HTTP round trip — re-serialising cookies, re-running middleware, re-parsing JSON — to obtain data that is already in memory

issues.summary: browse-logs casts request.json() directly to its typed shape while sibling routes validate before trusting the body
issues.suggestion: Mirror the fights/+server.ts pattern in browse-logs: read the body as `unknown`, narrow each field (`typeof params.bossId === 'number'`, etc.) and return `apiError('Invalid browse-logs body', 400)` on failure. If this validation pattern keeps recurring, add a `parseBrowseLogsBody()` next to parseFightRequestBody in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts (or a sibling validators module) so request-body parsing for /api routes lives in one place
evidence: src/routes/api/browse-logs/+server.ts: `const params: BrowseLogsParams = await request.json();` — no shape check, no try around the parse, the cast pretends the boundary is typed

src/routes/api/fights/+server.ts: `const body = (await request.json().catch(() => null)) as { code?: unknown } | null; ... if (typeof code !== 'string' || !code) return apiError('Invalid or missing report code.', 400);`
src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts exports parseFightRequestBody() and handleWclFightRequest() and 5 per-fight endpoints flow through them; browse-logs is the obvious sibling that opted out
A malformed POST to /api/browse-logs will throw an uncaught SyntaxError or coerce undefined fields into the SQL builder, where a sibling handler would have returned a clean 400

issues.summary: Core rating math (keystoneCalculations.ts) has no tests despite being the product of the rating-calculator route
issues.suggestion: Add src/lib/__tests__/keystoneCalculations.test.ts with table-driven cases for scoreFormula across keystone levels 2-30, all four affix breakpoints, and timed/depleted runs; assert exact numeric outputs and that computeRunLevelsForScore is the inverse for representative score targets.
evidence: src/lib/calculations/ contains a single file keystoneCalculations.ts that exports score formulas and computeRunLevelsForScore
No keystoneCalculations.test.ts exists in src/lib/__tests__/ or anywhere else under src/
Historical issues note bugs in this file (rebuilt lookup per call, unreachable trailing return) — exactly the kind of regression a unit suite should pin

issues.summary: All 22 SvelteKit API +server.ts handlers are untested — no request validation, error path, or upstream-failure coverage
issues.suggestion: Introduce a minimal SvelteKit handler-test harness: import the POST/GET export, build a `Request` with crafted JSON, mock getRaidDb()/getUserDb() and upstream fetch, then assert status code, response shape, and at least one validation-failure path per handler. Start with the highest-fan-in handlers (browse-logs, fights, player-details, recent-reports).
evidence: src/routes/api/ contains 22 +server.ts files, each parsing untrusted JSON via `await request.json()` and forwarding to DB or Warcraft Logs/Blizzard
There is no api/**/__tests__ directory and no test imports any +server.ts module
Mechanical scan flags all of these files with detector test_coverage alongside `smells` and `boilerplate_duplication`

issues.summary: OAuth and token-cache modules (Blizzard + base) have no tests
issues.suggestion: Add tokenCache.test.ts covering: valid-token short-circuit, expired-token refresh, fetch-failure path, and concurrent-refresh dedup. Add a small oauthBase test that stubs fetch and verifies request shape (grant_type, scope) and error propagation. These modules are pure functions over fake clocks/fetches and are cheap to test.
evidence: src/lib/auth/ has 6 modules; no corresponding test files exist
src/auth.ts and src/hooks.server.ts (CSP, cookie forwarding) are also untested
Token cache logic involves expiry math and refresh races that are silent failure modes if broken

issues.summary: performance.test.ts measures in-file fabricated workloads against wall-clock thresholds; tests nothing real and is timing-fragile
issues.suggestion: Delete performance.test.ts entirely. If real performance budgets are needed, replace with targeted benchmarks against actual hot paths (e.g. keystone score computation over a realistic batch of runs) using vitest's `bench` API rather than synthetic loops.
evidence: Test file defines its own `heavyComputation`, `processLargeDataset`, and `simulateApiCall` helpers (lines 41-75) and benchmarks them
Assertions use environment-dependent thresholds (<1000ms, <500ms, <200ms) and `console.log` the timings
No production module under src/ is imported or exercised

issues.summary: drizzleConfigs.test.ts re-states the literal config strings instead of validating behavior
issues.suggestion: Either delete the test (drizzle config is statically typed) or replace with one assertion that loads the schema referenced by the config and confirms it imports without error, which actually validates the wiring.
evidence: Test asserts `config.schema === './src/lib/db/schema.ts'`, `config.out === './drizzle'`, etc. — exact restatements of the config file
These assertions break on any legitimate path refactor without catching a real defect

issues.summary: formIndex.test.ts only verifies a barrel module re-exports its own symbols
issues.suggestion: Delete formIndex.test.ts. TypeScript already enforces export presence at build time; if alias parity matters, encode it as a type-level test (`type _ = typeof Field extends typeof FormField ? true : never`) or remove the alias surface.
evidence: All assertions are `expect(FormExports).toHaveProperty('Field')` and `expect(FormExports.FormField).toBe(FormExports.Field)`
Test couples 1:1 to the index file content; no behavior is exercised

issues.summary: Page and ContentShowcase tests assert exact marketing copy and Tailwind class fragments — high churn, low signal
issues.suggestion: Replace text-equality assertions with stable role/landmark queries (e.g. `getByRole('heading', { level: 1 })`); drop className assertions entirely (CSS regressions belong to visual review, not unit tests). Remove the fake-timer setup from contentShowcase tests since no timer is actually exercised.
evidence: page.test.ts: `expect(screen.getByText('Master World of Warcraft with Mr. Mythical'))` and `'Premium WoW Tools for Competitive Players'` — verbatim hero copy
contentShowcase.test.ts: `expect(link?.className).toContain('hover:scale-105')`, `'transition-transform'`, `image?.className.toContain('block')` — pure styling assertions
contentShowcase.test.ts wraps every test in `vi.useFakeTimers()` despite not advancing time in any assertion

issues.summary: dbIndex.test.ts only asserts a function exists, providing no real coverage of the data layer
issues.suggestion: Either delete dbIndex.test.ts or expand it to cover the real branches: throws when `building` is true, throws when neither DATABASE_USER_URL nor DATABASE_URL is set, returns a drizzle instance with a stubbed connection string. Use vi.mock for `postgres` and `$app/environment`.
evidence: Entire file body: `expect(typeof getRaidDb).toBe('function')`
userDb/getUserDb factory contains real branching (build-mode guard, missing env, postgres client construction) — none of it is exercised

issues.summary: No cross-module / load-function tests exist — server load functions and client store interactions are unverified
issues.suggestion: Add at minimum one integration test per major route group: instantiate the +page.server.ts load function with a mock `event`, stub the DB/fetch layer, and assert the returned data shape feeds the page component without runtime errors. This closes the gap between unit-tested helpers and untested wiring.
evidence: All 11 test files import only pure helpers or render single components in isolation
No test mounts a route with its load function output, no test exercises the currentState store's API persistence, and no test verifies the layout server's session bootstrap

add.header: Dual-database boundary documented at userSchema.ts
add.description: src/lib/db/userSchema.ts opens with a multi-paragraph doc comment naming both Postgres instances, both env vars, both drizzle configs, and both connection factories. The most surprising piece of architecture has its rationale written down at the source — newcomers can orient without asking.

add.header: Domain-aligned lib/ subfolders are now coherent
add.description: After the prior wontfix reorg of src/lib/utils, the subsystem folders (auth/, calculations/, data/, db/, server/, stores/, types/, ui/) each map to a clear capability with cohesive contents. lib/auth in particular shows intentional shared bases (oauthBase + provider files, tokenCacheBase + provider files).

add.header: db/connection.ts is an unused factory
add.description: createDrizzlePostgres exists, claims ownership of the shared client config in its doc comment, and has zero importers. db/index.ts and db/userDb.ts each inline the same config. Three modules now share ownership of one decision; the factory should either be adopted or removed.

add.header: db/index.ts barrel conflates the two databases
add.description: index.ts owns getRaidDb but also re-exports userSchema, users, userRecents, and getUserDb. The deliberate two-database boundary documented in userSchema.ts is then erased at the import surface.

add.header: README is empty; PRODUCT.md is the only orienting doc
add.description: README.md is 0 bytes. PRODUCT.md exists and is sharp on audience/purpose, but it does not describe code structure, the dual-DB split, the api route taxonomy, or the two component homes — orientation has to be reverse-engineered from imports.

add.header: Two component roots, undocumented
add.description: src/components/ holds feature components and src/lib/components/ui/ holds shadcn primitives. The split is workable and consistent in practice, but no README/DESIGN doc states the rule, and src/components/ violates the SvelteKit-convention default of $lib/components.

add.header: Twin utilities homes (lib/utils.ts vs lib/utils/)
add.description: A 14-line lib/utils.ts (shadcn cn helper) coexists with a lib/utils/ directory (clientLog.ts only). Same import root, same name — a textbook 'where do I put a new helper?' ambiguity.

add.header: Layered error spine: apiResponses + logger + dbOperation + WclQueryError
add.description: The server defines a deliberate four-piece error contract — apiError/apiOk for wire shape, logServerError/handleApiError for log+respond, dbOperation as the DB boundary, and WclQueryError for the upstream WCL boundary. The pieces are documented and used together for most of the API surface, which is why the codebase reads as designed rather than ad-hoc.

add.header: Two equivalent handler idioms compete
add.description: About half of the API routes use handleApiError and the other half manually pair logServerError + apiError to achieve the same effect. The wire output matches, but the duplication weakens the 'one canonical catch' grep-ability the helper was built for.

add.header: Errors-as-empty-success leak in character-reports
add.description: The aggregator endpoint converts both inner-source failures and its own top-level error into empty arrays inside a 200 response, so a fully-failed call looks identical to a user with no characters. This directly contradicts the documented dbOperation rule that errors must remain observable.

add.header: Logging-channel drift outside server/ and db/
add.description: Modules under lib/data, lib/stores, and lib/db/bnetAccount.ts emit free-form console.error strings instead of going through logServerError, breaking the [scope] log shape the rest of the server enforces. This is what causes 'inconsistent error logging' to keep recurring on scans.

add.header: Silent catches in data layer hide failures
add.description: warcraftlogs.ts, characterData.ts, and parts of bnetAccount.ts use bare or anonymous catches that return null with no log call, so genuine I/O failures are indistinguishable from 'not found'. The pattern is localized to a few files but affects user-visible flows like character lookup.

add.header: handle* is the Svelte event-handler dialect
add.description: The handle* prefix is used 10+ times across components and routes for DOM/event callbacks (handleSignIn, handleSelect, handleBossChange). This is an established framework convention in this codebase — the suffix always carries the actual intent — and should not be flagged as a generic verb.

add.header: Domain-rich nouns dominate utility naming
add.description: Utility and storage modules use highly specific names (raiderIoRealmCandidates, extractWarcraftLogsReportCode, keystoneCalculations, myWowCharacters). This makes greps and IDE navigation reliable and signals strong domain modelling discipline.

add.header: `response` is the project convention for fetch results
add.description: Across hooks.server.ts, oauthBase.ts, multiple chart components, and API routes the variable `response` is used 7+ times for awaited fetch values. Future reviewers should treat `res` as a drift outlier, not a neutral abbreviation.

add.header: Earned shared OAuth/token-cache factories
add.description: oauthBase.ts and tokenCacheBase.ts each have two real provider callers (WCL + Battle.net) and add concurrency-safe in-flight de-dup, so the indirection clearly pays for itself rather than being ceremonial.

add.header: WCL GraphQL handler factory used across event endpoints
add.description: handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts is consumed by cast/damage/healing/boss event handlers with distinct generic type pairs, so the generics and options bag are exercised polymorphically instead of being a single-use shell.

add.header: shadcn-svelte UI barrels are intentional facades
add.description: src/lib/components/ui/*/index.ts re-exports define a stable shadcn-style component package surface; they are explicitly out of scope per the rubric and should not be flagged as facade indirection.

add.header: death-events bypasses the WCL fight handler factory
add.description: death-events/+server.ts inlines parseFightRequestBody + try/catch + WclQueryError mapping rather than calling handleWclFightRequest, even though its shape matches the factory exactly — a single consistency outlier that prevents this dimension from scoring higher.

add.header: Unified protected-route seam
add.description: Every authenticated /api/* handler composes the same three pieces — requireSession (returns {session}|{response}), apiError/apiOk for response shape, and logServerError for telemetry. The seam is narrow, discriminated, and used uniformly across recent-characters, recent-reports, current-state, my-wow-characters, character-reports.

add.header: WCL fight endpoints share one boundary helper
add.description: handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts owns body validation, GraphQL execution, transform, and error translation for all 5 per-fight POST endpoints. Each route file is a query string + a transform — composition of collaborators, not glue.

add.header: Discriminated outcomes at store/backend seams
add.description: Backends (recentReportsBackends, recentCharactersBackends) return AddOutcome unions ('ok'|'unauthorized'|'unavailable') and data fetchers (characterData.ts) return {kind:'ok'|'empty'|'error',status} unions. Composition layers branch on the tag rather than inspecting payload shapes, keeping seam contracts explicit.

add.header: DB connection factory exists but is bypassed
add.description: src/lib/db/connection.ts createDrizzlePostgres documents itself as the single owner of the postgres client config, yet getRaidDb (db/index.ts) and getUserDb (db/userDb.ts) each inline the full config block. The seam was extracted but neither caller migrated, so the duplication persists.

add.header: Session resolved via self-HTTP in hooks.server.ts
add.description: handleSession reads the session by fetching `${event.url.origin}/auth/session` from inside the same SvelteKit server, hand-passing the cookie header, instead of calling Auth.js directly. Adds a network round-trip seam where an in-process call would suffice.

add.header: Tests cluster on pure helpers, miss critical paths
add.description: The few well-written tests (warcraftlogs report parser, ability metadata helpers, reveal action, cn) all target leaf-level pure functions. Every fan-in hub — keystone scoring, API handlers, OAuth, DB factories, server hooks — is untested, so the suite's green status doesn't reflect product correctness.

add.header: Self-referential and timing-fragile tests dilute the suite
add.description: performance.test.ts benchmarks in-file fixtures, drizzleConfigs/formIndex tests restate config/barrel literals, and component tests assert verbatim copy and Tailwind classes. These tests churn on cosmetic edits and never fail for real defects, training developers to ignore CI signal.

add.header: No integration or handler harness exists
add.description: There is no helper to invoke a SvelteKit +server.ts handler with a fabricated Request and mocked DB/fetch. Until that harness exists, adding API route tests has a high per-test setup cost, which is likely why none have been written.

add.header: Vitest + Testing Library are wired correctly
add.description: Component tests under src/components/__tests__ render Svelte components with @testing-library/svelte and vitest mocks (fake timers, MockIntersectionObserver) — the tooling is in place; the gap is in what gets tested, not how.
